nodes.name: Asymptotic Safety
nodes.definition: Scenario in which gravity is a non-perturbatively renormalizable quantum field theory because its renormalization-group flow terminates in the ultraviolet at a non-trivial (interacting) fixed point, keeping all dimensionless couplings finite and physical quantities 'safe' from divergences even without asymptotic freedom (g* != 0).

nodes.name: Black Hole Thermodynamics & the Information Paradox
Emergent & Entropic Gravity
nodes.definition: The thesis that the spacetime metric and its Einstein dynamics are not fundamental variables but a collective/effective, low-energy description of a deeper microscopic theory, analogous to how hydrodynamics or elasticity emerges from molecular physics.

nodes.name: Entanglement & Emergence of Spacetime (It from Qubit)
Group Field Theory & Tensor Models
nodes.definition: A quantum field theory for a field phi: G^d -> C (or R) defined over d copies of a Lie group G. Field arguments represent parallel transports on the d boundary faces of an elementary d-simplex; the interaction vertex glues d+1 fields into a d-simplex, so Feynman graphs are dual to simplicial complexes (triangulations) and Feynman amplitudes equal spin-foam amplitudes. The partition function is a non-perturbative sum over simplicial topologies and geometries.

nodes.name: QFT in Curved Spacetime & Semiclassical Gravity
nodes.definition: Moller-Rosenfeld: classical metric sourced by the expectation value of the quantum stress tensor, G_mu_nu = 8 pi G <T_mu_nu>. Its nonrelativistic one-particle limit is the nonlinear Schroedinger-Newton equation. A candidate for non-quantized gravity, but its nonlinearity threatens superluminal signaling and Born-rule violation.

nodes.name: Supergravity & UV Behavior of Perturbative Gravity
Swampland Program & the String Landscape
nodes.definition: Program (Vafa 2005) characterizing which low-energy effective field theories admit a UV completion in quantum gravity (the landscape) versus those that do not (the swampland). Central conjectures: Swampland Distance Conjecture, Weak Gravity Conjecture, de Sitter Conjecture, Emergent String Conjecture, plus the species scale as a gravitational cutoff.

nodes.name: Von Neumann Algebras & Modular Theory in Quantum Gravity
Reuter Fixed Point
nodes.definition: The interacting non-Gaussian UV fixed point of the gravitational RG flow, first found by Reuter (1998) in the Einstein-Hilbert truncation; UV-attractive in the relevant directions and the cornerstone of the asymptotic-safety program.

nodes.name: Wetterich Equation
nodes.definition: Exact functional flow equation for the effective average action, d_t Gamma_k = (1/2) STr[(Gamma_k^(2)+R_k)^-1 d_t R_k], implementing the Wilsonian RG by integrating out fluctuations shell-by-shell in momentum space; the central computational tool of functional RG quantum gravity.

nodes.name: Functional Renormalization Group
nodes.definition: Wetterich-equation-based non-perturbative RG flow applied to TGFTs to search for fixed points and phase transitions. In the cyclic-melonic potential approximation a U(1) TGFT is equivalent to an effective O(N) model and exhibits a continuous phase transition.

nodes.name: Effective Average Action
nodes.definition: Coarse-grained functional Gamma_k in which quantum fluctuations with momenta above the cutoff scale k have been integrated out; interpolates between the microscopic action (k->infinity) and the full effective action (k->0).

nodes.name: IR Regulator (cutoff function R_k)
nodes.definition: Momentum-dependent mass-like term R_k that suppresses fluctuations with p<k inside the Wetterich equation; its functional form (e.g. Litim optimized, exponential) is the chief origin of scheme dependence of fixed-point data.

nodes.name: Einstein-Hilbert Truncation
nodes.definition: Simplest non-perturbative approximation of the gravitational effective average action, retaining only the running Newton coupling and cosmological constant: Gamma_k ~ (1/16 pi G_k) int sqrt(g)(2 Lambda_k - R); yields the Reuter fixed point.

nodes.name: Dimensionless Couplings g and lambda
nodes.definition: g_k = k^(d-2) G_k (dimensionless Newton coupling) and lambda_k = k^-2 Lambda_k (dimensionless cosmological constant); the fixed point exists only for these dimensionless combinations.

nodes.name: Beta Functions
nodes.definition: Flow equations beta_i = d_t g_i of the dimensionless couplings, obtained from the trace in the Wetterich equation; their simultaneous zeros define RG fixed points.

nodes.name: Renormalization Group Fixed Point
nodes.definition: Point in theory space where all beta functions vanish; a Gaussian fixed point has free-field (canonical) scaling, a non-Gaussian one has anomalous critical exponents and can provide UV completion without asymptotic freedom.

nodes.name: Critical Exponents
nodes.definition: Eigenvalues theta_I of (minus) the stability matrix d beta_i/d g_j at a fixed point; Re(theta)>0 marks a relevant (UV-attractive) direction and Re(theta)<0 an irrelevant one. At the Reuter fixed point they form a complex-conjugate pair theta = theta' +/- i theta'' (e.g. -1.69 +/- 2.49i in the Einstein-Hilbert truncation, Percacci's review); high-order f(R) truncations report a convergent leading exponent of order theta' ~ 2.5.

nodes.name: UV Critical Surface
nodes.definition: Submanifold of theory space spanned by RG trajectories that emanate from the UV fixed point; its dimension equals the number of relevant directions and hence the number of free parameters, determining the predictivity of the theory (~3 relevant directions found).

nodes.name: Relevant Directions
nodes.definition: Eigendirections of the flow with Re(theta)>0 that grow toward the UV; their number equals the number of free parameters of an asymptotically safe theory. Polynomial f(R) truncations consistently find about three.

nodes.name: Predictivity
nodes.definition: The property that a finite-dimensional UV critical surface fixes infinitely many irrelevant couplings in terms of finitely many relevant ones, so that asymptotic safety makes definite predictions once a few parameters are measured.

nodes.name: Near-Canonical Scaling
nodes.definition: Empirical finding that quantum scaling dimensions of curvature operators R^n at the Reuter fixed point stay close to their canonical values, so only operators up to about R^2 are relevant, bounding the number of relevant directions to ~3 and supporting truncation convergence.

nodes.name: f(R) and Higher-Derivative Truncations
nodes.definition: Extensions of the Einstein-Hilbert ansatz to polynomial or functional f(R), R^2_munu, R^3 etc.; high-order polynomial f(R) studies (up to R^34 and beyond) display convergence of critical exponents and ~3 relevant directions.

nodes.name: Graviton Anomalous Dimension
nodes.definition: At the fixed point eta_N = 2-d (eta_N = -2 in d=4), changing the effective graviton propagator from 1/p^2 to 1/p^4; this UV softening underlies dimensional reduction of spacetime.

nodes.name: Spectral Dimension
nodes.definition: Effective dimension probed by diffusion, d_s = -2 d ln P(sigma)/d ln sigma. Many approaches (CDT, asymptotic safety, LQG, noncommutative geometry, Horava-Lifshitz) predict it runs from 4 at large scales to ~2 at the Planck scale ('dimensional reduction'), a candidate shared signature but phenomenologically hard to access.

nodes.name: Dimensional Reduction
nodes.definition: Scale-dependent lowering of the effective dimension of quantum spacetime from 4 at large scales to ~2 near the Planck scale, observed in CDT via the spectral dimension. Suggested as a mechanism for self-renormalizing UV gravity, shared with asymptotic safety and Horava-Lifshitz.

nodes.name: Weak-Gravity Bound
nodes.definition: Critical strength of gravitational fluctuations above which gravity generates complex fixed-point values in matter (scalar) interactions, destroying asymptotic safety; constrains the admissible matter content and favors spinning matter (fermions, vectors).

nodes.name: Matter-Gravity Systems
nodes.definition: Systems coupling minimally and non-minimally interacting matter (scalars, fermions, gauge fields) to asymptotically safe gravity; for a given number of gauge fields there is a maximal number of scalars and fermions compatible with the fixed point, and the Standard Model plus common extensions are found compatible.

nodes.name: Gravity-Induced Anomalous Dimension
nodes.definition: Contribution of gravitational fluctuations to the running of matter couplings; a positive value renders otherwise non-asymptotically-free couplings (Abelian gauge, quartic scalar) irrelevant and thus predictable, the mechanism behind SM coupling retrodictions.

nodes.name: Higgs Mass Retrodiction
nodes.definition: Shaposhnikov-Wetterich (2009) prediction that, assuming a positive gravity-induced anomalous dimension driving the quartic Higgs coupling to a near-zero fixed point at the Planck scale, the Higgs boson mass is M_H ~ 126 GeV with a few-GeV uncertainty, close to the later measured 125.25 GeV.

nodes.name: Top Mass Prediction
nodes.definition: Eichhorn-Held (2017) result that asymptotically safe gravity can fix the top Yukawa as an irrelevant coupling, yielding a top pole mass M_t ~ 171 GeV and a top-bottom mass difference of about 170 GeV.

nodes.name: Fine-Structure Constant Prediction
nodes.definition: Eichhorn-Held-Wetterich (2017) mechanism in which the balance of gravity and matter fluctuations creates an attractive IR fixed point for the Abelian gauge coupling, making the value of the fine-structure constant calculable for a class of GUT models.

nodes.name: Background Independence
nodes.definition: The requirement that a quantum theory of gravity employ no fixed, prior spacetime metric; the gauge group is Diff(M) and geometry is fully dynamical. Linked to the hole argument: diffeomorphic metrics are physically identical. There is no agreed precise definition (Anderson, Giulini, Pooley, Read 2023), which is itself a conceptual problem and a source of the LQG-vs-string-theory dispute.

nodes.name: Split / Modified Ward Identities
nodes.definition: Identities relating the dependence of the effective average action on the background metric and on the fluctuation field; their solution is required to restore background independence in functional RG quantum gravity.

nodes.name: Graviton Spectral Function
nodes.definition: Real-time (Lorentzian) spectral representation of the graviton propagator; recent self-consistent computations find a positive spectral function with a massless graviton peak, a multi-graviton continuum, near-quadratic UV decay and unit total spectral weight, providing evidence for unitarity.

nodes.name: Lorentzian Asymptotic Safety
nodes.definition: Formulation of the asymptotic-safety RG flow directly in Lorentzian (real-time) signature rather than via Euclidean computation plus Wick rotation; recent covariant and foliated approaches find a non-trivial fixed point in the Einstein-Hilbert truncation.

nodes.name: Unitarity
nodes.definition: Conservation of probability in the quantum theory; a central open question for AS because higher-derivative operators classically carry Ostrogradski ghosts. Recent evidence (UV-decreasing e+e- -> mu+mu- cross section, positive graviton spectral function) supports unitarity non-perturbatively.

nodes.name: 2+epsilon Expansion
nodes.definition: Weinberg's original perturbative evidence for asymptotic safety: near d=2, where Newton's constant is dimensionless, the beta function has a non-trivial fixed point g* ~ (3/38) epsilon with a single relevant direction; extrapolation to d=4 is uncontrolled.

nodes.name: Scheme / Truncation Dependence
nodes.definition: Dependence of fixed-point coordinates and individual critical exponents on the choice of gauge, regulator and truncation; only certain quantities (e.g. the product g* lambda*, leading exponents) are robustly universal, which is a main line of criticism.

nodes.name: Bekenstein-Hawking entropy
nodes.definition: Thermodynamic entropy of a black hole, S = A/(4 G) (in natural units), proportional to the horizon area A rather than volume. It is the target reproduced by microscopic state counting in string theory and the seed of the holographic principle.

nodes.name: Hawking radiation
nodes.definition: Quantum emission of (nearly) thermal radiation by a black hole at the Hawking temperature T_H = hbar kappa / (2 pi c k_B), with kappa the surface gravity. Derived via Bogoliubov mixing of in/out modes across the collapse; spectrum modified by greybody factors. Leads to evaporation (lifetime ~ M^3) and the information paradox.

nodes.name: Four laws of black hole mechanics
nodes.definition: Bardeen-Carter-Hawking analogues of thermodynamics: zeroth (kappa constant on horizon), first (dM = kappa/(8 pi G) dA + Omega dJ + Phi dQ), second (dA >= 0, Hawking area theorem), third (kappa cannot reach zero in finite steps).

nodes.name: Generalized second law (GSL)
nodes.definition: The statement that the generalized entropy S_matter + A/4G never decreases, extending the ordinary second law of thermodynamics to systems containing black holes. A cornerstone semiclassical result linking horizon area, Hawking radiation and quantum-field entropy.

nodes.name: Black hole information paradox
nodes.definition: If Hawking radiation is exactly thermal and the black hole fully evaporates, an initial pure state evolves into a mixed state, apparently violating quantum unitarity. Sharpened by Mathur's theorem: growing entanglement between radiation and hole cannot decrease without O(1) corrections at the horizon.

nodes.name: Page curve
nodes.definition: Time evolution of the fine-grained (von Neumann) entropy of Hawking radiation: for unitary evaporation it rises until the Page time (half the entropy radiated) then falls back to zero. Hawking's calculation gives monotonic growth instead - the conflict that defines the paradox.

nodes.name: Page time
nodes.definition: The moment when the fine-grained entropy of Hawking radiation reaches its maximum and begins to decrease, occurring roughly halfway through evaporation when about half the initial Bekenstein-Hawking entropy has been radiated.

nodes.name: Black hole complementarity
nodes.definition: Susskind-Thorlacius-Uglum proposal: an external observer sees information encoded in radiation on the stretched horizon while an infalling observer crosses smoothly; the two descriptions can never be operationally compared, so no contradiction arises.

nodes.name: Firewall (AMPS) paradox
nodes.definition: AMPS argument that purity of radiation, validity of effective field theory outside the horizon, and a smooth horizon cannot all hold, due to monogamy of entanglement; the most conservative resolution is that an infalling observer burns at a high-energy 'firewall' at the horizon.

nodes.name: Fuzzball / microstate geometry
nodes.definition: Mathur's proposal that a black hole with entropy S is described by exp(S) horizonless, singularity-free string/supergravity microstate geometries; the classical black hole is their coarse-grained average. Resolves the information paradox by removing the horizon (Bena-Martinec-Mathur-Warner microstate geometries program).

nodes.name: Soft hair
nodes.definition: Zero-energy BMS supertranslation/superrotation charges (soft gravitons/photons) on the horizon implying infinitely many conservation laws; Hawking-Perry-Strominger proposed they store information on a holographic plate at the horizon's future boundary. Later shown insufficient alone (Bousso-Porrati).

nodes.name: Quantum extremal surface (QES)
nodes.definition: Fine-grained entropy including quantum corrections: S = min ext [ Area(partial I)/4G + S_bulk(I union rad) ]. After the Page time an island in the black-hole interior dominates, the radiation entropy turns over, and the Page curve is recovered, restoring unitarity (Penington 2019; Almheiri et al. 2019).

nodes.name: Island formula
nodes.definition: Prescription for the entropy of Hawking radiation: S(Rad) = min ext over islands I of [Area(boundary I)/(4 G) + S_semicl(Rad ∪ I)]. Before Page time the optimal island is empty; after Page time a nontrivial island inside the horizon lowers the entropy, reproducing the Page curve.

nodes.name: Replica wormholes
nodes.definition: New gravitational saddle points connecting different replica copies in the replica-trick computation of the gravitational path integral. They dominate after the Page time and lead to the island rule; they also imply non-factorization of partition functions (baby-universe / ensemble issue).

nodes.name: Entanglement wedge reconstruction
nodes.definition: Bulk operators (including the black-hole interior) can be reconstructed from a boundary/radiation region once they lie in its entanglement wedge; realized as quantum error correction (Almheiri-Dong-Harlow) and the Petz map.

nodes.name: State dependence
nodes.definition: Papadodimas-Raju proposal that operators describing the black-hole interior depend on the (micro)state of the black hole rather than being universal CFT operators; geometrized by the Python's lunch and tied to evading the typical-state firewall.

nodes.name: Holography of information
nodes.definition: Raju et al. principle that in quantum gravity, all information in the bulk of an asymptotically flat (or AdS/dS) spacetime is also available in a small neighbourhood of its boundary, a sharp departure from local QFT. Formulated in AdS, flat, and de Sitter space.

nodes.name: ER=EPR
nodes.definition: Conjecture (Maldacena-Susskind 2013) that quantum entanglement (EPR) between two systems is equivalent to an Einstein-Rosen bridge (ER, wormhole) connecting them; a maximally entangled pair of black holes is a wormhole. Proposed resolution of the firewall paradox.

nodes.name: Holographic complexity
nodes.definition: Proposals (Complexity = Volume, Complexity = Action) relating the quantum computational complexity of a boundary state to bulk geometric quantities (volume of a maximal slice or action of a Wheeler-DeWitt patch), capturing late-time growth of the Einstein-Rosen bridge.

nodes.name: Scrambling time
nodes.definition: Time t_* ~ (beta/2pi) ln S for a black hole to delocalize injected information across its degrees of freedom; black holes are the fastest scramblers and saturate the chaos bound lambda_L <= 2 pi T/hbar.

nodes.name: Central dogma
nodes.definition: Working hypothesis of the modern program: from the outside a black hole behaves as a quantum system with A/(4G) degrees of freedom evolving unitarily.

nodes.name: Black hole remnant
nodes.definition: Hypothetical stable or long-lived Planck-scale object at the endpoint of evaporation that would store the information; suffers from infinite-production and infinite-degeneracy problems (Susskind).

nodes.name: Quantum error correction in holography
nodes.definition: Almheiri-Dong-Harlow: bulk locality in AdS/CFT is realized as a quantum error-correcting code, where a bulk operator is reconstructible on multiple boundary subsystems; the interior is encoded in early radiation.

nodes.name: Generalized entropy
nodes.definition: S_gen = <A>/4G_N + S_out, the sum of horizon area in Planck units and exterior matter entropy. In the crossed-product (type II) algebra of gravitationally dressed observables the renormalized von Neumann entropy of a semiclassical state equals S_gen up to an additive constant. Individually the area term diverges as G->0 and S_out diverges from horizon entanglement (type III), but their sum -- the generalized entropy -- is finite and well-defined. The central bridge object linking modular theory, black-hole thermodynamics and holographic entropy.

nodes.name: Ensemble averaging / baby universes
nodes.definition: Gravitational path integral with wormhole topologies appears to compute ensemble averages of theories (Saad-Shenker-Stanford: JT gravity = matrix integral); Marolf-Maxfield formalize this via alpha-states in a baby-universe Hilbert space, leading to the factorization puzzle.

nodes.name: Causal triangulation
nodes.definition: A simplicial spacetime glued from flat Minkowskian 4-simplices that admits a global foliation into spatial slices of fixed topology, with gluing rules preserving the foliation and causal (time) orientation. Forms the configurations summed in the CDT path integral.

nodes.name: Global time foliation (causality condition)
nodes.definition: The defining ingredient of CDT: spacetime is sliced by a discrete proper-time label t into spatial hypersurfaces of constant topology, restricting the sum-over-histories to globally hyperbolic, causal geometries. Enables a well-defined Wick rotation.

nodes.name: Regge calculus
nodes.definition: Discretization of general relativity (Regge 1961) in which curvature is concentrated in deficit angles around (d-2)-dimensional bones of a simplicial complex; the Einstein-Hilbert action becomes a function of simplex volumes and subsimplex counts. CDT is dynamical-triangulations Regge calculus with fixed edge lengths.

nodes.name: Dynamical triangulations
nodes.definition: Lattice quantum gravity in which all edge lengths are fixed and the dynamics resides entirely in the connectivity (which simplices are glued to which); the path integral becomes a sum over distinct triangulations. CDT is the causal (Lorentzian) variant.

nodes.name: Euclidean Dynamical Triangulations (EDT)
nodes.definition: Historical precursor summing over Euclidean (positive-definite) triangulations without causality. In 4D it is dominated by a crumpled phase (near-infinite Hausdorff dimension) and a branched-polymer phase (Hausdorff dimension ~2), neither with a good classical limit; the transition between them is first order.

nodes.name: (4,1) and (3,2) four-simplex types
nodes.definition: In 4D CDT two causal building blocks exist depending on how their five vertices split between adjacent time slices: (4,1)-simplices (and time-reflected (1,4)) and (3,2)-simplices (and (2,3)). Spacelike edges satisfy l_s^2=a^2, timelike edges l_t^2=-alpha a^2.

nodes.name: Asymmetry parameter Delta
nodes.definition: Bare coupling relating the squared lengths of timelike and spacelike edges (alpha = l_t^2/l_s^2). A purely Lorentzian/causal parameter absent in EDT; tuning Delta together with kappa_0 generates the CDT phase diagram and the physical de Sitter phase.

nodes.name: CDT Regge action
nodes.definition: The discretized (Wick-rotated) Einstein-Hilbert action S_eu = -(kappa_0+6 Delta) N_0 + kappa_4 (N_41+N_32) + Delta(2 N_41+N_32), with bare inverse Newton coupling kappa_0, cosmological coupling kappa_4, asymmetry Delta and simplex/vertex counts N_0,N_41,N_32.

nodes.name: Wick rotation (CDT)
nodes.definition: Analytic continuation alpha -> -alpha turning complex Lorentzian weights e^{iS} into real positive Euclidean Boltzmann weights e^{-S_eu}. Well-defined in CDT because of the global foliation, enabling sign-problem-free Monte Carlo simulation.

nodes.name: Gravitational path integral / partition function
nodes.definition: Regularized nonperturbative sum-over-histories Z = sum_T (1/C(T)) e^{iS[T]}, Wick-rotated to Z = sum_T (1/C(T)) e^{-S_eu[T]}, where C(T) is the automorphism (symmetry) factor. The continuum theory is sought as a scaling limit a->0.

nodes.name: Monte Carlo simulation of quantum geometry
nodes.definition: Metropolis-algorithm sampling of Wick-rotated causal triangulations using local geometry-changing moves, allowing direct measurement of invariant quantum observables (volumes, dimensions, curvature) at a Planckian regime.

nodes.name: Hausdorff dimension
nodes.definition: Dimension defined by the scaling of volume with geodesic distance, V(r) ~ r^{d_H}. In the CDT de Sitter phase d_H ~ 4, confirming genuine four-dimensionality at large scales, in contrast to EDT phases (d_H = infinity or ~2).

nodes.name: CDT phase diagram (A, B, C_dS, C_b)
nodes.definition: The space of bare couplings (kappa_0, Delta) splits into four phases: A (branched polymer), B (crumpled), C_dS (de Sitter), and C_b (bifurcation). The A-C transition is first order; the B-C / C_dS-C_b transitions are second/higher order and candidate UV-continuum points.

nodes.name: De Sitter phase (C_dS)
nodes.definition: Physically interesting CDT phase whose average spatial-volume profile V_3(t) ~ cos^3(t/B) matches a round Euclidean four-sphere (de Sitter space), with semiclassical quantum fluctuations described by a minisuperspace effective action.

nodes.name: Bifurcation phase (C_b)
nodes.definition: Fourth CDT phase (discovered ~2016-2017) characterized by singular vertices of very high order; in the transfer-matrix description the kinetic term bifurcates into two Gaussians. The B-C_b transition is higher/second order.

nodes.name: Crumpled phase
nodes.definition: EDT (and CDT phase B) configuration with near-infinite Hausdorff dimension and maximal connectivity; lacks a sensible classical continuum geometry.

nodes.name: Branched-polymer phase
nodes.definition: EDT (and CDT phase A) configuration resembling a tree of thin tubes with Hausdorff dimension ~2; conformal-mode/baby-universe dominated, with no extended 4D geometry.

nodes.name: Emergence of de Sitter universe
nodes.definition: Central CDT result: a macroscopic 4D universe with de Sitter (cos^3) volume profile emerges dynamically from purely Planckian quantum fluctuations of the path integral, the only QG approach to demonstrate this. Measured universes span ~17-28 Planck lengths.

nodes.name: Minisuperspace effective action
nodes.definition: Effective action for the global scale factor / 3-volume, S_eff = (1/Gamma) sum [ (N_3(t+1)-N_3(t))^2/N_3(t) + delta N_3^{1/3}(t) ], measured directly in CDT and matching the Hartle-Hawking minisuperspace model, with sqrt(V_4)/Gamma = sqrt(V_4)/(24 pi G).

nodes.name: Transfer matrix
nodes.definition: Operator <n_{t+1}|M|n_t> ~ exp(-L_eff[n_t,n_{t+1}]) evolving spatial geometries between adjacent time slices, in 4D CDT parametrized solely by the 3-volume N_3(t). Reconstructs the effective minisuperspace action at all scales and the ground-state wave function.

nodes.name: Lines of constant physics / RG flow
nodes.definition: Trajectories in bare coupling space defined by semiclassical properties of the emergent universe, providing a notion of renormalization-group flow in a background-independent theory; the second-order transition line can be read as a UV line under anisotropic space-time scaling.

nodes.name: UV fixed point (continuum limit)
nodes.definition: A second- or higher-order critical point at which a nontrivial continuum limit (UV completion) of CDT might exist, identified with the Reuter fixed point of the asymptotic-safety scenario. Current Monte Carlo data allow but do not prove its existence.

nodes.name: Quantum Ricci curvature (QRC)
nodes.definition: Curvature observable (Klitgaard & Loll 2018) built from local distance and volume measurements only, needing no tensor calculus or smooth structure; reproduces ordinary Ricci curvature in the smooth limit. In CDT it is compatible with a four-sphere at large scales.

nodes.name: Effective topology / topological fingerprint
nodes.definition: Characterization of strongly fluctuating quantum geometry via Betti numbers of coarse-grained geometry as a function of coarse-graining scale (topological data analysis), yielding a 'topological fingerprint'; thin necks/pinchings recall minimal-neck baby universes (minbus).

nodes.name: Diffeomorphism invariance
nodes.definition: Physical observables must have vanishing Poisson brackets with all constraints (be constant along gauge orbits), so they cannot be local fields at manifold points or regions. Forces observables to be global or relational, a feature inherited directly from classical GR.

nodes.name: Matter coupling in CDT
nodes.definition: Adding fields to dynamical geometry: scalar fields on the triangulation (whose large amplitudes drive geometry transitions), and gauge fields coupled via the discretized Laplace-Beltrami / Hodge operator and spectral observables (Clemente & D'Elia 2023; Yang-Mills with U(1), SU(2) in 2D). Fermions remain obstructed by a sign problem.

nodes.name: Pachner moves (Monte Carlo updates)
nodes.definition: The local geometry-changing updates (in 4D the 1<->5, 2<->4, 3<->3 moves) that, modified to preserve the foliation and causal orientation, generate the Markov chain over causal triangulations; they must be ergodic and satisfy detailed balance.

nodes.name: Conformal-mode problem
nodes.definition: The Euclidean Einstein-Hilbert action is unbounded below along conformal rescalings (Gibbons-Hawking-Perry), making the naive Euclidean path integral divergent; in EDT this drives the pathological crumpled/branched-polymer phases. CDT's causal foliation effectively suppresses this mode, yielding a stable extended phase.

nodes.name: Causal Set (causet)
nodes.definition: A locally finite partially ordered set (poset) (C, <=) whose order relation is reflexive, acyclic (antisymmetric), and transitive, with every order interval I[x,y] = Fut(x) ∩ Past(y) finite. Elements model fundamental spacetime events; the order encodes proto-causality and local finiteness encodes intrinsic discreteness. No metric, topology, or differentiable structure is assumed.

nodes.name: Order plus Number equals Geometry
nodes.definition: Sorkin's slogan summarizing causal set theory: the causal (order) structure of spacetime fixes the conformal geometry (9/10 of the metric in 4D, via Hawking-King-McCarthy / Malament), while counting elements (number) supplies the conformal/volume factor; together they recover the full Lorentzian geometry.

nodes.name: Fundamental Spacetime Discreteness
nodes.definition: The hypothesis that spacetime is fundamentally discrete at the Planck scale, with a finite number of spacetime atoms in any finite volume. In CST discreteness is postulated and physical (one element per Planck 4-volume, rho = l_p^-4), not derived as in LQG or a mere regulator as in CDT.

nodes.name: Local Finiteness
nodes.definition: The axiom that every order interval I[x,y] = {z : x <= z <= y} contains finitely many elements. This is the precise mathematical statement of discreteness in CST and distinguishes a causal set from a general (continuum) causal order.

nodes.name: Order Interval (Alexandrov interval)
nodes.definition: The set I[x,y] = Fut(x) ∩ Past(y) of all elements causally between x and y. Its cardinality is the discrete analogue of the spacetime volume of the Alexandrov interval between two events; abundances of small order intervals build the Benincasa-Dowker action and dimension estimators.

nodes.name: Number-to-Volume Correspondence
nodes.definition: The relation <n> = rho V: the expected number of causal set elements in a spacetime region equals the discreteness density times its 4-volume. Supplies the volume element missing from pure causal (conformal) structure, completing the recovery of the Lorentzian metric.

nodes.name: Hawking-Malament Theorem
nodes.definition: Theorem (Hawking-King-McCarthy 1976, Malament 1977) that for a strongly causal spacetime the causal (conformal) structure determines the metric up to a local conformal factor — '9/10 of the metric' in 4D. Mathematical foundation of CST: order fixes conformal geometry, only the volume scale is missing.

nodes.name: Sprinkling (Poisson process)
nodes.definition: Procedure generating a causal set embedded in a given spacetime (M,g) by a Poisson point process at density rho: points placed at random uniformly w.r.t. the volume measure, inheriting the order from the manifold causal structure. The unique discretization preserving Lorentz invariance on average (no preferred frame).

nodes.name: Hauptvermutung (fundamental conjecture)
nodes.definition: Conjecture that a causal set can be faithfully embedded at a given density into two spacetimes (M,g) and (M',g') if and only if they are approximately isometric — i.e. a causet determines its continuum spacetime up to approximate isometry. Not fully proven; central obstruction is defining a distance/closeness on Lorentzian geometries.

nodes.name: Lorentz Invariance of Discreteness
nodes.definition: Theorem (Bombelli-Henson-Sorkin 2009) that a Poisson sprinkling into Minkowski space admits no equivariant way to single out a preferred direction/frame; fundamental discreteness in CST does NOT break local Lorentz invariance. Key advantage over regular lattices, which always pick a frame.

nodes.name: Poisson Distribution of Sprinkled Elements
nodes.definition: The number of elements n in a region of volume v is Poisson-distributed: P_v(n) = (rho v)^n exp(-rho v)/n!, mean rho v, standard deviation sqrt(rho v). The sqrt-N number fluctuations drive Sorkin's cosmological constant prediction.

nodes.name: Benincasa-Dowker Action
nodes.definition: Combinatorial discrete analogue of the Einstein-Hilbert action for causal sets (Benincasa-Dowker 2010), built from the discrete d'Alembertian / scalar curvature operator. In 4D: S/hbar = N - N1 + 9 N2 - 16 N3 + 8 N4, where Ni counts inclusive order intervals of cardinality i. Converges in mean to the EH action.

nodes.name: Discrete (nonlocal) d'Alembertian
nodes.definition: A one-parameter family of retarded, Lorentz-invariant but nonlocal linear operators B on scalar fields on a causal set, summing over past layers L1..L4 with dimension-dependent coefficients. Its sprinkling average converges to (Box - R/2)phi, extracting the Ricci scalar.

nodes.name: Inherent Nonlocality
nodes.definition: Because Lorentz invariance forbids a finite set of nearest neighbours (the proper-time hyperbola crosses infinitely many sprinkled points), any covariant operator must be nonlocal, summing over many layers. Nonlocality is forced by discreteness + Lorentz invariance, complicating QFT but enabling phenomenology (swerves, off-shell dark matter).

nodes.name: Classical Sequential Growth (CSG)
nodes.definition: Rideout-Sorkin (1999) stochastic dynamics in which a causal set grows element by element, each new element born to the future of some subset, constrained by discrete general covariance and Bell causality. A classical 'halfway house' to quantum gravity; transitive percolation is its simplest one-parameter model.

nodes.name: Transitive Percolation
nodes.definition: Simplest CSG model: each new element is linked to each existing element independently with probability p, then transitive closure is taken. A one-parameter renormalizable cosmological model exhibiting scaling and a fixed point; testbed for continuum recovery.

nodes.name: Discrete General Covariance
nodes.definition: Requirement in CSG that physical predictions are independent of the (arbitrary) labelling/order in which elements are born — the discrete analogue of diffeomorphism invariance / general covariance. Together with Bell causality it strongly constrains the growth probabilities.

nodes.name: Bell Causality
nodes.definition: Condition in CSG that the birth probability of a new element depends only on its causal past, not on causally disconnected elements; a discrete causality requirement preventing superluminal influence. With discrete general covariance it nearly fixes the form of CSG transition probabilities.

nodes.name: Everpresent Lambda (cosmological constant fluctuation)
nodes.definition: Sorkin's 1987 heuristic prediction that the cosmological constant fluctuates around zero with magnitude Lambda ~ 1/sqrt(V) ~ 1/sqrt(N) ~ 10^-120 in Planck units, from a Lambda-V uncertainty relation in unimodular gravity and Poisson volume fluctuations Delta V ~ sqrt(N). The only successful prediction-in-advance from a quantum gravity theory.

nodes.name: Unimodular Gravity (Lambda-V conjugacy)
nodes.definition: Gravity with the constraint sqrt(-g)=const, so the cosmological constant appears as an integration constant rather than a Lagrangian coupling, offering a new vantage on the cosmological-constant problem; classically equivalent to GR, with subtle quantum (in)equivalence.

nodes.name: Myrheim-Meyer Dimension
nodes.definition: Dimension estimator for a causal set from the ordering fraction r = <R>/binom(N,2) (fraction of causally related pairs), since <R>/<N>^2 = f0(d) depends only on the Minkowski dimension d. Higher dimension => less ordering. KR posets give d_MM ~ 2.38.

nodes.name: Sorkin-Johnston (SJ) Vacuum
nodes.definition: Covariant, uniquely defined vacuum state for a free scalar field in a bounded globally hyperbolic region (or causal set), built from the positive part of the spectrum of the Pauli-Jordan operator i*Delta. Requires no choice of time slice or mode expansion; central to QFT on causal sets.

nodes.name: Pauli-Jordan Operator
nodes.definition: The operator i*Delta = i(G_R - G_A), the difference of retarded and advanced Green functions, which on a finite causal set is a finite antisymmetric matrix. Its eigendecomposition defines the SJ vacuum and the spacetime entanglement entropy; the field commutator.

nodes.name: Quantum Field Theory on a Causal Set
nodes.definition: Scalar field theory defined directly on a causal set via the retarded propagator / Pauli-Jordan matrix, the SJ vacuum, and Peierls bracket — bypassing canonical quantization. Recent work extends to in-in/in-out correlators, diagrammatics, and an S-matrix analogue for early-universe observables.

nodes.name: Spacetime Entanglement Entropy (SSEE)
nodes.definition: Sorkin's covariant spacetime formulation of entanglement entropy from a generalized eigenproblem W v = mu (i Delta) v, giving S = sum mu ln|mu|. On causal sets it obeys a volume law; an area law is recovered only after a UV truncation of the Pauli-Jordan spectrum.

nodes.name: Swerves (momentum diffusion)
nodes.definition: Phenomenological effect (Dowker-Henson-Sorkin 2004): a massive particle propagating on a causal set does not follow an exact geodesic but undergoes Lorentz-invariant diffusion in momentum space, governed by one parameter k. Named after Lucretius' clinamen; constrains the discreteness scale from cosmic-ray / nebular observations.

nodes.name: Off-shell Dark Matter from Nonlocality
nodes.definition: Saravani-Aslanbeigi-Afshordi proposal that the causal-set-inspired nonlocal d'Alembertian, in its continuum limit, yields a continuum of massive off-shell modes in addition to the usual massless field — a candidate cold dark matter relic of spacetime nonlocality/discreteness.

nodes.name: Quantum Measure / Anhomomorphic Logic
nodes.definition: Sorkin's histories-based generalized measure theory for quantum dynamics without a wavefunction: a decoherence functional D(A,A') gives a quantum measure mu(A)=D(A,A) that is non-additive due to interference, with the 'coevent' (anhomomorphic logic) interpretation for single closed systems — Sorkin's preferred route to quantum CST dynamics.

nodes.name: Causal Set Path Integral / Path-Sum
nodes.definition: Quantum dynamics as a sum over causal sets weighted by exp(i S_BD / hbar). Lorentzian (oscillatory) and lacking Wick rotation; studied via complex/Monte Carlo methods. Central arena for the entropy problem and continuum emergence.

nodes.name: Kleitman-Rothschild (KR) Posets
nodes.definition: The entropically dominant random posets: three-layer causal sets with ~n/4 elements in bottom and top layers and ~n/2 in the middle, numbering ~2^(n^2/4). They overwhelm the sample space yet look nothing like a manifold ('three moments of time'), defining the CST entropy problem.

nodes.name: Continuum Emergence / Suppression of Non-manifoldlike Sets
nodes.definition: The program of showing that the path integral dynamically suppresses non-manifoldlike causal sets (especially KR posets) so that manifoldlike spacetime emerges. Carlip et al. (2017-2024) show almost all non-manifoldlike sets are strongly suppressed, but the surviving class is not yet fully understood.

nodes.name: Black Hole Entropy as Causal Links (horizon molecules)
nodes.definition: Dou-Sorkin (2003) kinematic estimate: counting causal links crossing a black hole horizon near a hypersurface gives a number proportional to the horizon area, so each 'horizon molecule' carries ~1 bit per Planck area, reproducing Bekenstein-Hawking entropy S = A/4.

nodes.name: Causal Set Cosmology
nodes.definition: Cosmological models from CSG: cyclic bounce universes with 'cosmological renormalization' of dynamical parameters at each Big Crunch-Big Bang bounce, plus the everpresent-Lambda scenario with a fluctuating cosmological term tracking the critical density at each epoch.

nodes.name: Covariant Observables Problem
nodes.definition: The problem of constructing physically meaningful, label-independent observables in a coordinate-free, generally covariant discrete theory; partially solved for CSG (stem sets, covariant events) but incomplete for general/quantum dynamics.

nodes.name: Problem of time
nodes.definition: In canonical quantum gravity the Hamiltonian is a sum of constraints that annihilate physical states (H psi = 0), so there is no external time against which the state of the universe evolves. Encompasses the Hilbert-space problem (no canonical positive inner product), the multiple-choice/global-time problem, and the interpretation of probabilities in a timeless theory. Isham's classification: time before quantization (deparametrization), time after quantization (Page-Wootters), and timeless approaches.

nodes.name: Wheeler-DeWitt equation
nodes.definition: The quantum super-Hamiltonian constraint H psi[h_ab] = 0 acting on a wave functional on superspace (the space of 3-geometries). Contains no i hbar d/dt term, making the wave function of the universe formally static. Plagued by operator-ordering ambiguity, regularization, and ill-definedness of functional differential operators. The DeWitt supermetric G_abcd has Lorentzian signature, giving the scale factor the role of an intrinsic time.

nodes.name: Hamiltonian (scalar) constraint
nodes.definition: The constraint generating time reparametrizations in the ADM formulation of GR; together with the diffeomorphism (momentum) constraints it forms the Dirac/hypersurface-deformation algebra with structure functions. Its vanishing on physical states is the origin of the problem of time. In LQG it becomes the master constraint or Thiemann's quantum Hamiltonian.

nodes.name: Deparametrization
nodes.definition: Selecting a physical field (massless scalar, pressureless dust, universe volume / scale factor) as a clock and solving the scalar constraint for the clock momentum to obtain a true (non-vanishing) physical Hamiltonian generating relational evolution. Works cleanly in minisuperspace and for matter reference fields; fails globally due to turning points and Gribov ambiguities (global time problem).

nodes.name: Page-Wootters relational time
nodes.definition: A global timeless state satisfying H_tot |Psi>> = 0 is conditioned on the reading of a clock subsystem; the conditioned state |psi(t)> = <t|_C |Psi>> obeys an effective Schroedinger equation. Time emerges as a correlation between clock and system rather than as an external parameter ('evolution without evolution'). Criticized by Kuchar (propagators, multiple clocks), revived post-2015 and extended to non-ideal clocks via covariant POVMs.

nodes.name: Trinity of relational dynamics
nodes.definition: Hoehn-Smith-Lock equivalence of three formulations of relational quantum dynamics via quantum reduction maps: (i) relational Dirac observables in the clock-neutral picture of Dirac quantization, (ii) the Page-Wootters Schroedinger picture, (iii) the relational Heisenberg picture from symmetry reduction. Shows the apparently competing solutions to the problem of time are three faces of the same dynamics.

nodes.name: Hole argument
nodes.definition: Einstein's argument (revived by Stachel, Earman, Norton) that if the metric is determined only up to diffeomorphism, then spacetime points have no independent physical identity; only relations are physical. Gryb and Thebault argue it is structurally linked to the problem of time.

nodes.name: Relational / dressed observables
nodes.definition: Gauge-invariant (Dirac) quantities encoding 'the value of field f when clock field T equals tau', constructed as a power series of nested Poisson brackets with the constraint. Equivalently fields 'dressed' by a gravitational Wilson line or a material reference frame to make them diffeomorphism-invariant; such observables are intrinsically nonlocal. Recently linked to crossed-product von Neumann algebras and observer degrees of freedom.

nodes.name: Partial vs complete observables
nodes.definition: Rovelli's distinction: partial observables (metric, coordinate distances) are gauge-dependent but measured in practice; complete observables are gauge-invariant relational combinations. The conjecture that partial observables of a subsystem emerge as complete observables of the full system gauge-fixed explains why gauge-dependent quantities describe correct phenomenology. Mathematically developed by Dittrich for full GR.

nodes.name: Quantum reference frames
nodes.definition: Reference systems that are themselves quantum and may be in superposition; changes of QRF are 'superpositions of coordinate transformations'. Initiated by Giacomini-Castro-Ruiz-Brukner. Central to relational quantum theory and to defining gravitational entropy: a QRF is mathematically a crossed product that converts a type III to a type II algebra.

nodes.name: Crossed-product observer algebra
nodes.definition: Adjoining an observer's clock (with bounded-below Hamiltonian) to the type III_1 von Neumann algebra of QFT observables in a gravitational subregion yields a type II crossed-product algebra A_QFT x R, whose density matrices have well-defined entropy equal to the generalized entropy S_gen = A/4G + S_out. Realizes the observer/QRF as the structure that renders gravitational entropy finite and observer-dependent (CLPW 2022).

nodes.name: von Neumann algebra type (II vs III)
nodes.definition: Murray-von Neumann classification of factors by the range of dimensions/traces of their projections. Type I (B(H), ordinary QM, pure states, density matrices) has a trace with discrete range; type II has a continuous trace but no minimal (rank-one) projections, so it possesses density matrices and well-defined relative entropy but no pure states or 'bits'; type III has no trace at all and no density matrices, so entanglement entropy is intrinsically UV-divergent. The type is the precise mathematical fingerprint of how entanglement and entropy behave in a system.

nodes.name: Cosmological constant problem
nodes.definition: Quantum field theory predicts a vacuum energy density up to ~120 orders of magnitude (~60 after enforcing Lorentz invariance) larger than the observed dark-energy value Lambda ~ 10^-122 M_Pl^4. The most severe naturalness/fine-tuning problem in physics, connecting UV vacuum energy to IR cosmology.

nodes.name: Weinberg no-go theorem
nodes.definition: Weinberg (1989): no local field theory including classical gravity can have a flat (Lambda=0) solution for generic parameter values without fine-tuning; adjustment mechanisms via a scalar field generically fail (the field runs to zero or no static solution exists). Extended to quantum gravity (2017); evaded only by nonlocal gravity, anthropics, or unknown symmetry.

nodes.name: Vacuum / zero-point energy
nodes.definition: The zero-point energy of quantum fields that should gravitate and contribute to Lambda. Its calculated magnitude vastly exceeds observation, and its cutoff-dependence (vs the observed cutoff-independent Lambda) is the core of the cosmological constant problem. Sorkin's causal-set prediction Lambda ~ +/- 1/sqrt(V) gives the right order of magnitude from fluctuations.

nodes.name: Penrose-Hawking singularity theorems
nodes.definition: Under generic energy conditions and causality assumptions, GR predicts geodesic incompleteness (singularities). The theorems rely on classical energy conditions that may fail in quantum regimes, motivating but not guaranteeing singularity resolution by quantum gravity.

nodes.name: Geodesic incompleteness
nodes.definition: The defining technical content of a singularity: timelike or null geodesics that cannot be extended to arbitrary affine parameter, signaling breakdown of the classical spacetime description (not necessarily curvature blow-up).

nodes.name: Singularity resolution
nodes.definition: Replacement of a classical singularity by a region of large-but-finite curvature or a non-singular bounce in a more complete theory. Loop quantum cosmology gives a robust big bounce at Planckian curvature in symmetry-reduced models; species-scale arguments show some singularities (BTZ) instead worsen, while null singularities can resolve. Whether resolution survives in full inhomogeneous gravity is open.

nodes.name: Big bounce
nodes.definition: In loop quantum cosmology the big-bang singularity is replaced by a quantum bounce when curvature reaches ~Planckian values; the bounce is generically asymmetric (pre- and post-bounce branches differ, effective constants such as Newton's G can be rescaled). Provides an explicit example of singularity resolution.

nodes.name: Gravitationally induced collapse
nodes.definition: The hypothesis that superpositions of distinct mass distributions, being superpositions of incompatible spacetime geometries, are unstable and collapse. Connects the measurement problem directly to gravity. Realized by Penrose (objective reduction) and Diosi (stochastic localization with diffusion and radiation emission).

nodes.name: Penrose objective reduction
nodes.definition: A spatial superposition collapses in a time tau ~ hbar/E_G, where E_G is the gravitational self-energy of the difference between the two mass distributions. For an electron tau exceeds the age of the universe; for a near-Planck-mass dust grain (~10^-8 kg) collapse occurs in ~1 s. Provides an observer-free resolution of measurement via gravity.

nodes.name: Diosi-Penrose model
nodes.definition: A gravity-related objective wavefunction-collapse model in which a superposition of distinct mass distributions decoheres on a timescale tau ~ hbar/E_G, where E_G is the gravitational self-energy of the difference of the two mass distributions. Provides a testable prediction distinguishing classical-gravity collapse from unitary quantum gravity.

nodes.name: Spontaneous emission test of collapse
nodes.definition: Collapse-induced diffusion accelerates charged particles, producing detectable spontaneous radiation. The Gran Sasso underground experiment measured this rate in germanium/lead and ruled out the parameter-free Diosi-Penrose model, raising the lower bound on R_0 by ~3 orders of magnitude. The most sensitive class of tests is non-interferometric (diffusion/emission).

nodes.name: Measurement problem in quantum gravity
nodes.definition: If the entire universe including observers is quantum and evolves unitarily, who or what performs measurements and selects definite outcomes? Decoherence relocates rather than solves the problem (it needs a pre-defined pointer basis and a trace over an external environment), which is unavailable for a closed universe.

nodes.name: Role of the observer
nodes.definition: The observer is required both to define classical outcomes (measurement) and, algebraically, to render gravitational entropy finite (crossed product). In relational/QRF approaches all physics is observer-relative; gravitational entropy is observer-dependent. The observer becomes a dynamical, gravitating degree of freedom rather than an external classical agent.

nodes.name: Emergence of classicality
nodes.definition: How a smooth Lorentzian spacetime with sharp geodesics and an arrow of time emerges from a timeless, nonlocal, fundamentally quantum (or discrete) description. Mechanisms invoked: decoherence, decoherent/consistent histories, semiclassical limit, gravitational collapse. Decoherence presupposes a subsystem split that is not itself background-independently defined.

nodes.name: Decoherence
nodes.definition: Environment-induced suppression of interference that explains the appearance of classicality without solving the measurement problem (still requires a pointer basis and a partial trace). In quantum gravity the environment and the subsystem split are ambiguous.

nodes.name: Decoherent / consistent histories
nodes.definition: Gell-Mann-Hartle-Griffiths-Omnes framework assigning probabilities to coarse-grained histories of a closed system without an external measurement axiom; well-suited to reparametrization-invariant theories and quantum cosmology where there is no physical time coordinate. Halliwell applies it to the problem of time via spacetime coarse-grainings and the arrival-time problem.

nodes.name: Does gravity need quantizing?
nodes.definition: The open question of whether gravity must be a quantum field. Arguments for: Eppley-Hannah (1977, refuted), Belenchia-Wald et al. (2018, causality+complementarity), general consistency. Arguments for agnosticism: semiclassical gravity, Oppenheim's consistent post-quantum classical gravity. Decisive evidence awaits BMV entanglement or spacetime-diffusion experiments.

nodes.name: Eppley-Hannah argument
nodes.definition: A 1977 thought experiment claiming classical gravity coupled to quantum matter must either violate the uncertainty principle or permit superluminal signaling, hence gravity must be quantized. Widely refuted (Mattingly: the device cannot be built even in principle; Kent; Huggett-Callender), so it does not establish the necessity of quantization.

nodes.name: BMV gravity-induced entanglement witness
nodes.definition: Bose et al. and Marletto-Vedral (2017): two ~10^-14 kg masses each in spatial superposition, coupled only via gravity, become entangled only if gravity is non-classical, because local operations and classical communication cannot create entanglement. Observing entanglement would witness the quantum nature of (linearized) gravity. Challenged in 2025-26 by claims that classical gravity can also entangle.

nodes.name: Classical-gravity-entanglement debate (2025-26)
nodes.definition: Aziz-Howl (Nature 2025) claim classical gravitational interaction can transmit quantum information and generate entanglement by physically local processes, undermining BMV as a clean quantum-gravity test. Marletto-Oppenheim-Vedral-Wilson (2025) and Gundhi et al. counter that the model either produces no entanglement (discarded amplitudes) or mediates it via quantized matter, not gravity. Unresolved; hinges on the definition of a local mediator and local tomography.

nodes.name: Schroedinger-Newton equation
nodes.definition: Nonlinear nonrelativistic equation in which a particle's wavefunction feels its own Newtonian self-gravity through the |psi|^2 mass density. Used to model gravitationally induced localization and to test whether gravity alone can account for classicality.

nodes.name: Stochastic gravity
nodes.definition: Extension of semiclassical gravity that incorporates fluctuations of the stress tensor via the noise kernel, leading to the Einstein-Langevin equation with a Gaussian stochastic source. Captures the second moment <T T> and, to leading order in large N, reproduces the quantum correlation functions of gravitons coupled to N matter fields.

nodes.name: Post-quantum classical gravity
nodes.definition: A consistent framework coupling a fundamentally classical, stochastically fluctuating spacetime metric to quantum matter, evading no-go theorems by abandoning reversibility. Predicts unavoidable metric diffusion and gravitational decoherence, experimentally constrained by mass-localization (diffusion) measurements.

nodes.name: Decoherence-diffusion trade-off
nodes.definition: Any consistent (Markovian, CPTP) classical-quantum dynamics obeys a trade-off in which the metric diffusion coefficient D_2 is bounded below by the back-reaction coupling D_1 and the inverse decoherence (Lindblad) coefficients D_0 (Oppenheim et al. 2023, Eq. 23: 2 D_2 >= D_1 D_0^-1 D_1^dagger). Long coherence times (small decoherence) force strong metric diffusion, giving a double experimental squeeze: better interferometry implies more diffusion, in tension with noise measurements.

nodes.name: Spacetime diffusion
nodes.definition: Stochastic fluctuation of the metric required if gravity is fundamentally classical. Parametrized by a diffusion coefficient bounded by LISA Pathfinder, torsion balances, atom interferometry and nanomechanical resonators. Atom interferometry pushes below the ultralocal discrete model bound; the nonlocal continuous model survives by ~1 order of magnitude.

nodes.name: Locality vs unitarity tension
nodes.definition: For an old black hole, unitarity of Hawking evaporation demands strong correlations of late radiation with early radiation, while horizon smoothness demands maximal entanglement with interior partners, violating entanglement monogamy (firewall). Resolutions (islands/replica wormholes, ER=EPR, holography) preserve unitarity by sacrificing locality.

nodes.name: Minisuperspace
nodes.definition: A finite-dimensional truncation of superspace (e.g. homogeneous FLRW with scale factor a and scalar phi) where the Wheeler-DeWitt equation becomes a single solvable equation. The laboratory for deparametrization, the problem of time, no-boundary/tunneling wave functions and the measure problem.

nodes.name: Induced gravity (Sakharov)
nodes.definition: Sakharov's 1967 idea that the Einstein-Hilbert term arises as a one-loop effective action of quantum matter fields on a curved background; Newton's constant and the cosmological constant are determined by the UV cutoff and field spectrum (metric elasticity of the vacuum).

nodes.name: Metric elasticity
nodes.definition: Sakharov's analogy in which curvature shifts the zero-point energy of the vacuum, producing a stiffness of space proportional to the Ricci scalar R, just like stress in an elastic continuum.

nodes.name: Einstein equation as equation of state
nodes.definition: Jacobson's 1995 result that G_{munu}+Lambda g_{munu}=8 pi G T_{munu} is a macroscopic thermodynamic consistency condition (an equation of state) for horizon thermodynamics rather than a fundamental equation of motion.

nodes.name: Clausius relation delta Q = T dS
nodes.definition: Thermodynamic relation between heat flux across a horizon and entropy change; the engine of Jacobson's derivation when imposed for all local causal horizons.

nodes.name: Local Rindler / causal horizon
nodes.definition: For every point and null direction there exists a locally accelerating observer with a horizon, to which one assigns Unruh temperature T = hbar a / 2 pi c k_B and entropy S = eta A.

nodes.name: Unruh temperature
nodes.definition: A uniformly accelerated (Rindler) observer with proper acceleration a perceives the Minkowski vacuum as a thermal bath at temperature T_U = hbar a / (2 pi c k_B). Bogoliubov coefficients between Rindler and Minkowski modes yield an exact Bose-Einstein distribution; equivalent to the KMS condition at inverse temperature 2pi/a. Demonstrates the observer-dependence of the particle concept.

nodes.name: Holographic principle / screen
nodes.definition: Conjecture that the number of fundamental degrees of freedom describing physics in a spatial region is bounded by the area of its boundary in Planck units (N ~ A/4 l_P^2), not its volume; a full quantum-gravity description lives on a lower-dimensional boundary. Proposed by 't Hooft (1993) and Susskind (1994).

nodes.name: Entropic force
nodes.definition: A macroscopic force F = T dS/dx driving a system toward higher-entropy configurations (like polymer elasticity); Verlinde's 2010 mechanism by which Newtonian gravity and Einstein equations are recovered.

nodes.name: Equipartition law
nodes.definition: E = (1/2) N k_B T: each surface (horizon) degree of freedom carries (1/2) k_B T of energy; used by Verlinde and Padmanabhan to define horizon temperature and recover Newton's law.

nodes.name: Holographic equipartition (Padmanabhan)
nodes.definition: In any static spacetime N_sur = N_bulk; the departure from this equality, dV/dt = L_P^2 (N_sur - N_bulk), drives cosmic expansion and reproduces the Friedmann equation.

nodes.name: Emergence of cosmic space
nodes.definition: Padmanabhan's proposal that the expansion of the universe is the quest of spacetime to reach holographic equipartition; the Hubble volume evolves so that surface and bulk degrees of freedom equalize.

nodes.name: CosMIn (cosmic information)
nodes.definition: A dimensionless conserved count of modes crossing the Hubble radius across the three cosmic epochs; the single postulate N = 4 pi reproduces the observed cosmological constant Lambda l_P^2 approx 3.4e-122.

nodes.name: Entanglement equilibrium (Jacobson 2015)
nodes.definition: Hypothesis that vacuum entanglement entropy of a small geodesic ball is maximal at fixed volume in a locally maximally symmetric state; stationarity of total entropy is equivalent to the semiclassical Einstein equation.

nodes.name: Modular Hamiltonian
nodes.definition: The self-adjoint generator K = -log Delta of the modular flow sigma_t = Delta^{it} (.) Delta^{-it}. For a thermal/reduced state it plays the role of an entanglement (or 'entanglement') Hamiltonian; modular flow is generically nonlocal, but for the Rindler wedge in the vacuum (Bisognano-Wichmann) it is the local Lorentz boost and K = 2 pi (boost generator), giving the Unruh temperature. Variations of expectation values of K control the first law of entanglement and relative entropy.

nodes.name: Volume-law entropy of de Sitter
nodes.definition: In de Sitter space the thermal entropy of the cosmological horizon, spread through the volume, gives a term proportional to V that overtakes the area law beyond the Hubble scale; source of Verlinde's extra dark gravity.

nodes.name: Apparent dark matter (Verlinde 2016)
nodes.definition: Not a particle but an elastic, memory-like response of the dark-energy entropy to baryonic matter; produces extra gravity reproducing flat rotation curves and MOND-like behaviour with a_0 = c H_0.

nodes.name: MOND (Modified Newtonian Dynamics)
nodes.definition: Empirical modification of gravity below an acceleration scale a_0 yielding flat rotation curves; Verlinde's emergent gravity reproduces the MOND formula for point masses but differs (factor ~2) for extended bodies.

nodes.name: Radial acceleration relation (RAR)
nodes.definition: Tight empirical correlation between observed and baryonic acceleration in galaxies with negligible intrinsic scatter; used by Lelli et al. to challenge Verlinde's emergent gravity, which predicts intrinsic thickness.

nodes.name: Analogue gravity
nodes.definition: Framework in which perturbations (sound, light, quasiparticles) in a moving fluid or BEC propagate on an emergent effective curved metric; lets laboratory systems mimic horizons and Hawking radiation.

nodes.name: Acoustic metric / dumb hole
nodes.definition: Unruh's effective metric for sound in a flowing fluid; a sonic (dumb-hole) horizon forms where flow speed equals sound speed |v| = c_s, with an associated analogue Hawking temperature from the surface gravity (velocity gradient).

nodes.name: Fermi point (Volovik)
nodes.definition: A topologically stable defect in momentum space (as in superfluid 3He-A) near which relativistic fields, gauge symmetry and effective gravity emerge from the quantum vacuum.

nodes.name: Gravity as hydrodynamics / geometro-hydrodynamics
nodes.definition: View (Hu, Volovik) that general relativity is the long-wavelength, low-energy hydrodynamic/kinetic limit of an underlying many-body quantum system, with semiclassical gravity one rung above.

nodes.name: Non-equilibrium spacetime thermodynamics
nodes.definition: Eling-Guedens-Jacobson 2006 extension where dS = delta Q/T + d_i S with an internal entropy production term (bulk/shear viscosity of the horizon), required to obtain f(R) gravity and interpreted as tidal heating.

nodes.name: Gravity from quantum relative entropy (Bianconi)
nodes.definition: Bianconi's 2024/2025 action given by the quantum relative entropy between the spacetime metric and the metric induced by matter; an auxiliary G-field (Lagrange multipliers) yields a dressed Einstein-Hilbert action with an emergent small positive Lambda.

nodes.name: Entanglement entropy
nodes.definition: Von Neumann entropy S_A = -Tr(rho_A ln rho_A) of the reduced density matrix of a subregion A; measures quantum correlations between A and its complement. In QFT it obeys an area law with a UV cutoff and is the central quantity translated into geometry in holography.

nodes.name: Ryu-Takayanagi formula
nodes.definition: S_A = Area(gamma_A)/4G_N: entanglement entropy of a boundary region equals the area of the bulk minimal codimension-2 surface anchored on the boundary of A and homologous to A. Geometrizes entanglement.

nodes.name: HRT covariant entanglement entropy
nodes.definition: Covariant generalization of Ryu-Takayanagi to time-dependent states: the bulk surface is extremal (not minimal) in the full Lorentzian geometry, with the smallest-area extremum chosen; reduces to RT on static slices.

nodes.name: FLM quantum correction
nodes.definition: Faulkner-Lewkowycz-Maldacena one-loop quantum correction to RT: S_A = Area/4G_N + S_bulk, where S_bulk is the entanglement entropy of bulk fields across the RT surface.

nodes.name: Thermofield double (TFD)
nodes.definition: Entangled pure state of two CFT copies whose bulk dual is the two-sided eternal AdS black hole; the Einstein-Rosen bridge length grows with time, motivating complexity = volume / action proposals.

nodes.name: Entanglement builds geometry (Van Raamsdonk)
nodes.definition: Thesis that classically connected spacetime emerges from quantum entanglement; disentangling the degrees of freedom of two regions makes them pull apart and pinch off, quantified by standard entanglement measures.

nodes.name: First law of entanglement
nodes.definition: delta S_A = delta <H_A>: to first order in perturbations, the change in entanglement entropy equals the change in expectation value of the modular Hamiltonian H_A; the exact quantum analog of dE = T dS. For ball-shaped regions H_A is a local integral of the stress tensor.

nodes.name: Einstein equations from entanglement
nodes.definition: Faulkner et al. 2014: the set of first laws of entanglement for all ball-shaped boundary regions is equivalent, via RT, to the linearized Einstein equations about pure AdS; nonlinear extension recovers full Einstein equations at second order. Gravity emerges from entanglement thermodynamics.

nodes.name: JLMS relation
nodes.definition: Jafferis-Lewkowycz-Maldacena-Suh: the boundary (CFT) modular Hamiltonian of a region equals the bulk modular Hamiltonian of its entanglement wedge plus the area operator over 4G, K_bdy = A/4G + K_bulk. Boundary modular flow is dual to bulk modular flow. Recast algebraically, JLMS is the statement that the area operator is the central/affiliated operator distinguishing the type II crossed-product entropy from the bulk type III relative entropy; it makes generalized entropy the von Neumann entropy of the boundary algebra.

nodes.name: Holographic quantum error correction
nodes.definition: Bulk logical qubits are redundantly encoded into boundary physical qubits; explains subregion duality, radial commutativity and the resilience of bulk reconstruction to boundary erasures.

nodes.name: HaPPY code
nodes.definition: Holographic tensor-network code of perfect tensors (pentagons/hexagons) on a hyperbolic lattice (Pastawski-Yoshida-Harlow-Preskill 2015); an exactly solvable toy model of AdS/CFT obeying RT and negative tripartite information.

nodes.name: MERA tensor network
nodes.definition: Multiscale entanglement renormalization ansatz for critical ground states; its emergent radial (RG-scale) direction is interpreted as a discrete slice of AdS (Swingle 2012), realizing holographic geometry from entanglement renormalization.

nodes.name: Tensor network holography
nodes.definition: General program building emergent bulk geometry from tensor networks whose link structure encodes entanglement; includes MERA, HaPPY, hyperinvariant networks, and lifted/random networks reproducing RT.

nodes.name: Bit threads
nodes.definition: Max-flow/min-cut reformulation of RT (Freedman-Headrick 2016): S_A is the maximum flux of a divergence-free, norm-bounded vector field through A; Planck-thickness threads represent bilateral entanglement and naturally implement monogamy and nesting.

nodes.name: Holographic entropy cone
nodes.definition: The set of linear inequalities (subadditivity, strong subadditivity, monogamy of mutual information, and higher-party inequalities) characterizing which entropy vectors are realizable by RT/HRT minimal surfaces; a necessary condition for a state to have a classical geometric dual. The covariant (HRT) cone was proven equivalent to the static (RT) cone in 2026 via a no-short-cut theorem.

nodes.name: Wheeler-DeWitt patch
nodes.definition: Bulk spacetime region whose gravitational action equals the boundary state's complexity in the complexity=action conjecture; bounded by null sheets from the boundary slice.

nodes.name: Quantum focusing conjecture (QFC)
nodes.definition: Bousso-Wall conjecture that the quantum expansion (the derivative of generalized entropy per unit area along a null congruence) is non-increasing. Unifies the Bousso bound with classical focusing at the semiclassical level and implies the quantum null energy condition.

nodes.name: Quantum null energy condition (QNEC)
nodes.definition: A local bound stating that the null energy density at a point is bounded below by the second variation of the entanglement entropy of a region whose boundary passes through that point. Proven from relative entropy/modular flow and implied by the quantum focusing conjecture; the semiclassical replacement of the classical null energy condition.

nodes.name: Covariant entropy (Bousso) bound
nodes.definition: Entropy on a light-sheet does not exceed A/4G_N; the generalized (quantum) Bousso bound follows from QFC.

nodes.name: Traversable wormhole
nodes.definition: An Einstein-Rosen bridge rendered traversable by negative average null energy from a double-trace deformation coupling the two boundaries (Gao-Jafferis-Wall 2017); interpreted via ER=EPR as quantum teleportation; cannot violate causality.

nodes.name: Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model
nodes.definition: Quantum mechanics of N Majorana fermions with random all-to-all q-body interactions in (0+1)D. In the large-N limit it is dominated by melonic diagrams, develops an emergent conformal (reparametrization) symmetry in the IR with operator dimension Delta=1/q, saturates the chaos bound with Lyapunov exponent lambda_L = 2 pi/beta, and is holographically dual to near-AdS2 / Jackiw-Teitelboim gravity.

nodes.name: Schwarzian action
nodes.definition: Low-energy effective action S = -(N alpha_S/J) integral {f,tau} dtau of the reparametrization (soft) mode f(tau) describing the spontaneous + explicit breaking of conformal symmetry in SYK at large but finite coupling. It is maximally chaotic and is the boundary mode of near-AdS2 dilaton (Jackiw-Teitelboim) gravity.

nodes.name: Near-AdS2 / JT gravity holography
nodes.definition: Duality between nearly-AdS2 Jackiw-Teitelboim gravity and the Schwarzian sector of SYK at low temperature; the simplest controllable model of emergent holography and gravitational dynamics.

nodes.name: Chaos bound (Lyapunov)
nodes.definition: Maldacena-Shenker-Stanford bound lambda_L <= 2pi/beta on the Lyapunov growth of out-of-time-order correlators; saturated by black holes and the SYK model (maximal chaos).

nodes.name: von Neumann algebra crossed product / type II
nodes.definition: Including the gravitational constraint changes the algebra of observables in a subregion from type III_1 to type II, permitting a well-defined (renormalized) entropy; gives an algebraic origin of A/4G and a framework for islands, firewalls and emergent time via modular flow.

nodes.name: Subregion-subalgebra duality
nodes.definition: The holographic correspondence between a boundary subregion's algebra and the bulk algebra of its entanglement wedge: bulk operators are reconstructed as elements of the boundary subalgebra (entanglement-wedge reconstruction), and modular flows match (JLMS). Phrased algebraically it makes the von Neumann type and center of the subalgebra the carrier of bulk geometric information (wedge, horizon, area operator).

nodes.name: Emergent time
nodes.definition: Open problem of deriving the time coordinate (not just spatial directions) from quantum information; approached via half-sided modular flow, Connes cocycle, and pseudo-entropy / timelike entanglement in cosmological and de Sitter holography.

nodes.name: Pseudo-entropy / timelike entanglement
nodes.definition: Generalization of entanglement entropy to non-Hermitian transition density matrices; generically complex-valued, used as a probe of the emergence of time in cosmological and de Sitter holography.

nodes.name: Modified Dispersion Relation (MDR)
nodes.definition: A Planck-suppressed deformation of E^2 = p^2 c^2 + m^2 c^4 of the form E^2 = p^2 c^2 [1 +/- (E/E_QG)^n], with n=1 (linear) or n=2 (quadratic) and E_QG the sought quantum-gravity energy scale. Implies an energy-dependent photon group velocity v(E) = c[1 -/+ ((n+1)/2)(E/E_QG)^n]. Core object of all time-of-flight phenomenology.

nodes.name: Lorentz Invariance Violation (LIV)
nodes.definition: Explicit breaking of Lorentz symmetry by a preferred frame/scale, systematically parametrized by Standard-Model Extension (SME) coefficients organized by operator mass dimension (4,5,6,...). Dimension-5 and -6 operators generate modified dispersion and vacuum birefringence. Constrained by GRB time-of-flight, UHECR thresholds, neutrinos, and polarization.

nodes.name: Doubly/Deformed Special Relativity (DSR) and kappa-Poincare
nodes.definition: A quantum-gravity-motivated deformation of special relativity adding a second invariant scale (E_Pl) WITHOUT a preferred frame, realized algebraically by the kappa-Poincare Hopf algebra. Implies relative locality. Crucially, unlike LIV some DSR models predict NO observable time-of-flight delay for massless particles.

nodes.name: Relative Locality
nodes.definition: Principle replacing absolute locality in DSR/curved-momentum-space models: coincidence of events is observer-dependent, with curvature in momentum space at the Planck scale. Has the consequence that energy-dependent arrival-time delays can vanish despite a deformed dispersion relation, complicating LIV interpretation of GRB data.

nodes.name: Time-of-Flight Delay
nodes.definition: Energy-dependent difference in arrival time of photons (or neutrinos) emitted simultaneously from a distant source, accumulated over cosmological distance: Delta t ~ ((n+1)/2)(E_h^n - E_l^n)/E_QG^n times a redshift integral. Non-observation yields lower bounds on E_QG. Primary observable for Fermi-LAT and LHAASO GRB studies.

nodes.name: Vacuum Birefringence
nodes.definition: For a CPT-odd (dimension-5) LIV operator, left- and right-circular polarizations propagate at different speeds, rotating the linear polarization vector by an energy-dependent angle Delta theta ~ xi (E^2-E0^2) d / (2 E_Pl). High observed polarization of distant GRBs excludes large xi; more sensitive than time-of-flight by ~1/omega, giving the tightest propagation bound (xi < ~2.4e-15).

nodes.name: Threshold Anomaly
nodes.definition: LIV shifts the energy thresholds of particle reactions (GZK photopion production p + gamma_CMB -> Delta; pair production gamma gamma -> e+ e-), distorting observed UHECR and TeV gamma-ray spectra and the cosmogenic neutrino flux. A complementary, source-model-independent LIV probe.

nodes.name: UHECR and Neutrino LIV Constraints
nodes.definition: Ultra-high-energy cosmic rays (Auger) and astrophysical neutrinos (IceCube) constrain LIV: LIV would suppress GZK photopion interactions, modify the highest-energy spectrum, and shift the cosmogenic neutrino peak. Energy-dependent neutrino-photon delays can reach M_QG >~ 1e26 eV (linear) / 1e19 eV (quadratic) in principle.

nodes.name: Gravitationally Induced Entanglement (GIE / QGEM)
nodes.definition: Generation of entanglement between two spatially superposed masses via their mutual gravitational interaction (Bose et al., Marletto-Vedral 2017). Argued to witness the quantum nature of gravity, but recent work shows classical/post-quantum gravity models can also produce it, weakening theory-independent no-go claims.

nodes.name: Bose-Marletto-Vedral (BMV) Experiment
nodes.definition: Concrete GIE realization: two ~1e-14 kg masses (NV-center nanodiamonds) placed in adjacent Stern-Gerlach matter-wave interferometers, separated by >~200 um (to suppress Casimir-Polder), held in superposition ~250 um wide for ~1 s; a spin entanglement witness certifies gravity-mediated entanglement.

nodes.name: Gravitational Phase (BMV)
nodes.definition: The relative phase Delta phi = G m^2 Delta t / (hbar d) acquired between interferometer branches due to the Newtonian potential difference; entanglement is detectable when Delta phi reaches O(1) rad within the coherence time. For m~1e-14 kg, d~200 um, Delta t~1 s this is order radians.

nodes.name: Two-Mode Squeezing (gravitational entangler)
nodes.definition: Expansion of the Newtonian interaction yields a bilinear coupling H_int ~ (G m1 m2 / d^3) x1 x2, a two-mode squeezing Hamiltonian that entangles the positions/momenta of the two masses. The mechanism underlying GIE even in variants without spin-based Stern-Gerlach splitting.

nodes.name: Entanglement Witness
nodes.definition: An observable whose value outside a classical bound certifies the presence of entanglement. In BMV, spin correlations such as <sigma_x^(1) sigma_y^(2)> witness gravity-mediated entanglement without full state tomography.

nodes.name: Gravitational Decoherence
nodes.definition: Loss of quantum coherence induced by gravity: either fundamental objective collapse (Diosi-Penrose) or universal decoherence from gravitational time dilation (Pikovski et al.). Sets the dominant limit on BMV-type experiments and is itself a testable quantum-gravity-adjacent prediction.

nodes.name: Universal Decoherence from Time Dilation
nodes.definition: Pikovski-Zych-Costa-Brukner mechanism: gravitational time dilation couples a composite particle's internal energy to its center of mass, decohering its position without any external environment. Earth's g already suffices for micrometre objects, offering a gravitational route to the quantum-to-classical transition.

nodes.name: Holographic Noise
nodes.definition: Hogan's conjecture that transverse positions of bodies fluctuate randomly by ~Planck length per Planck time (Planckian geometric indeterminacy), giving a flat (frequency-independent) strain power spectral density set only by the Planck time. Measured by the Fermilab Holometer (two cross-correlated 40 m Michelson interferometers).

nodes.name: Generalized Uncertainty Principle (GUP)
nodes.definition: Deformation Delta x Delta p >= (hbar/2)(1 + beta (Delta p)^2 + ...) implying a minimal measurable length Delta x_min = beta_0 ell_Pl. Common to string theory, LQG, and noncommutative geometry; constrained in macroscopic oscillators, interferometers, and LIGO (currently beta_0 <~ 1e6-1e10).

nodes.name: Minimal Length
